Facts.text: Felix III begins his reign as Catholic Pope
12th recorded perihelion passage of Halley's Comet
Battle of Badr: Muhammad's Muslim forces win a significant victory over the Meccan army
Spanish fleet occupies Djerba near Tripoli
Influential politician Cardinal Granvelle flees Brussels
Battle of Oosterweel: Spanish mercenary troops destroy a band of Dutch rebels near Antwerp, start of the Eighty Years' War
Battle at Tondibi: Moroccan army under Judar [Jawdar] defeats Sultan Askia Ishaq II of Songhai
First meeting of what would become the Academie Francaise in Paris at the house of Valentin Conrart
Cambridge College, Massachusetts, renamed Harvard for clergyman John Harvard
Jews are denied the right to build a synagogue in New Amsterdam
Massachusetts gains title to Maine for $6,000
1st US Moravian bishop, David Nitschmann, consecrated in Germany
27th recorded perihelion passage of Halley's Comet
Gotthold Lessing's "Emilia Galotti" premieres in Brunswick
France informs Great Britain of their Treaty of Alliance with the US; Great Britain responds with a declaration of war against France four days later
John Martin, 1st American-born actor, performs in Philadelphia
Luigi Cherubini's opéra-comique "Médée" premieres at the Théâtre Feydeau in Paris, France
Friedrich Hebbel's play "Maria Magdalena" premieres in Königsberg
Uncle Sam cartoon figure debuts in the New York Lantern Weekly
Arkansas legislature passes anti-Ku Klux Klan law
Oxford University defeats Cambridge University in their 1st golf match
Siege of Khartoum, Sudan, begins by Mahdist forces and lasts ten months
Great Blizzard of 1888 rages across the east coast of the USA and Canada
J. L. Johnstone of England invents the starting gate for horse racing
San Diego State University is founded.
British troops occupy Bloemfontein, capital of the Orange Free State (Boer War)
In France the length of the working day for women and children is limited by law to 11 hours.
Amidst increasing anti-gambling sentiment, wagering on horse racing is banned in San Francisco, CA; Ingleside Race Track closes March 15
Fall of the Sokoto Caliphate in Northern Nigeria, British claim supremacy over 500,000 square miles
Bronze statue of Christ on Argentine-Chilean border dedicated
Ivan Caryll's musical "The Pink Lady" premieres at the New Amsterdam Theatre, NYC; runs for 336 performances
Stanley Cup, Dey's Arena, Ottawa, ON: Ottawa Senators beat Galt (ON), 7-4
The Colonial-Born and Settlers Indian Association is formed at a meeting in Durban, South Africa, and has at its aim to fight the infamous 3 poll tax
Bulgaria and Serbia conclude an alliance pact ostensibly against Austria, but it secretly provides for a possible war against Turkey
Stanley Cup, Quebec Skating Rink, Quebec City, Quebec: Quebec Bulldogs rout Moncton Victorias (NB), 8-0 for a 2-0 sweep of the challenge series
Kansas legislature approved censorship of motion pictures
Brooklyn Robins manager Wilbert Robinson tries to catch a baseball dropped from an airplane, but the pilot substitutes a grapefruit
American Red Magen David (Jewish Red Cross) forms
After the German government is forced to cut its army to 10,000 men, military groups plot an unsuccessful coup - a revolt ended by a general strike
Mongolia (formerly Outer Mongolia) declares independence from China
WRR-AM in Dallas TX begins radio transmissions
American inventor Lee de Forest demonstrates his sound-on-film moving pictures (NYC)
Tennessee makes it unlawful to teach evolution
Rudolph Friml's musical "Three Musketeers" premieres in NYC
Clyde Tombaugh announces the discovery of Pluto at Lowell Observatory
American banks allowed to reopen after a government imposed bank holiday
Driving tests introduced in Great Britain
Romanian composerGeorge Enescu's opera "Œdipe", inspired by the mythological tale of "Oedipus the King" premieres in Paris, with French libretto by Edmond Fleg
In a process known as Anschluss, Austria is annexed into Nazi Germany
World News Roundup is broadcast for the first time on CBS Radio in the United States.
Finland-Russian cease fire signed, the Winter War ends. Finland gives up Karelische
Fons de Boungne and Corneil Dombret found AGRA (Amis du Grand Reich Allemand (Friends of the Greater German Reich)) in Begium
Julia Flikke of the Nurse Corps, becomes 1st woman colonel in US army
Baseball approves official ball (with cork & balata)
Frank Dixon wins Knights of Columbus mile (4:09.6)
Nazi Sicherheitsdienst arrests Dutch resistance fighter Henry Werkman
Paul-Henri Spaak (Belgium Socialist Party) forms Belgian government - then shortest Belgian government (ends 31 March)
19th Academy Awards: "Best Years of Our Lives", De Havilland, March win
2nd Dutch government of Willem Drees forms
Israel demands DM 6.2 billion compensation from Germany
Bir BSD Mahendra succeeds Tribhubana as king of Nepal
NZ bowl out WI for 77 at Eden Park to score their 1st Test Cricket win
Government troops land in Sumatra Indonesia
LPGA Titleholders Championship Women's Golf, Augusta CC: Fay Crocker of Uruguay wins by 7 strokes ahead of Kathy Cornelius
NFL's Chicago Cardinals moves to St Louis
White Sox unveil new road uniforms with players' names above number
Elizabeth Gurley Finn (70) becomes President of US Communist Party
Landslide in USSR, kills 145
Old type, black & white notes cease to be legal tender
Yugoslavia grants 1,000 prisoners amnesty
Indonesia & Netherlands reinstate diplomatic relations
Two Russian reconnaissance flights over Alaska
Turkey threatens Cyprus with armed attack
The Beatles' "Eight Days a Week" single goes #1 and stays #1 for 2 weeks
Congo sentences ex-premier Moise Tsjombe to death
Robert Anderson's "You Know I Can't Hear You ..." premieres in NYC
Beatles release single "Lady Madonna" in the UK
Nerve gas accident at Skull Valley, Utah, kills 6,000 sheep
Apollo 9 returns to Earth
100 year Beehive anniversary ends in brawl in Amsterdam
Digital Equipment Corp introduces PDP-11 minicomputer
San Francisco city employees begin 4-day strike
Minskoff Theater opens at 200 W 45th St NYC
Syria adopts constitution
Glenn Turner scores twin tons for NZ's 1st win against Aust
Moluccan "suicide commandos" occupies Province house
European Monetary System is established, ECU created
Gairy dictatorship in Grenada overthrown by New Jewel Movement
Ford Motor Co found innocent in death of 3 women in a fiery Pinto
Ice Pairs Championship at Copenhagen won by Baess & Thierbach (GDR)
Worlds Ladies' Figure Skating Champs in Copenhagen won by Elaine Zayak (USA) who lands 6 triple jumps
1st USFL overtime game-Birmingham Stallions beat Oakld Invaders 20-14
Last day of 1st-class cricket for G Chappell, R Marsh, B Laird
Michael Secrest (US) begins 24-hr ride of 516 miles, 427 yards
Microsoft has its initial public offering and lists on the NASDAQ
Soyuz T-15 carries 2 cosmonauts to Soviet space station Mir
Ice Dance Championship at Cincinnati won by Bestemianova & Bukin (URS)
Washington Caps score 5 goals against Toronto in 3 mins & 3 secs
Japan's Seikan Tunnel, the world's longest tunnel with an underwater segment (53.90 km in total) opens, connecting Honshu-Hokkaido by rail. The Channel Tunnel remains the longest underwater tunnel.
27th shuttle, Discovery 8, launched, 1st woman to do the countdown
FDA orders recall of all Chilean fruit in US
US space shuttle STS-29 launched
Nicholoas Braithwaite elected premier of Grenada
Exxon pays $1-billion dollars in fines & cleanup of Valdez oil spill
Saudi Arabia and Iran say OPEC oil production cuts will take effect April 1
FCC rules companies can own 30 AM & 30 FM stations (formerly 12)
Magnitude 6.7 earthquake strikes near Erzincan in eastern Turkey, killing over 500 people
Blizzard of '93 hits north-east USA
A third of voters in Austria vote for the far-right Freedom Party
Oil tanker Nassia is hit by the cargo ship the Shipbroker in the Bosporus Strait, causing a huge fire, the deaths of 24 crew members, injuries to 29 and 10 missing
President Mangope of Bophuthatswana deposed
Anti-fascist Kazakhstan anti-parliament forms
Hungarian Forint devalued 9%
Istanbul police shoot dead 16 Alawite demonstrators
At Dunblane Primary School, Scotland, 16 children and 1 teacher are shot dead by Thomas Hamilton who then commits suicide. Results in handguns being banned in the UK.
Sri Lanka beats India in a Cricket World Cup semi-final as riots stop play at Eden Gardens in Calcutta
India's Missionaries of Charity chooses Sister Nirmala to succeed Mother Teresa as its leader
Phoenix lights seen at night over Phoenix, Arizona by hundreds of people, and by millions on television. Now a hotly debated controversy.
The journal Nature reports that 350,000-year-old footprints of an upright-walking human have been found in Italy
Terry Ratzmann shoots and kills seven members of the Living Church of God, including the minister, at Sheraton Inn in Brookfield, Wisconsin, before killing himself
Gold prices on the New York Mercantile Exchange hit $1,000.00 an ounce for the first time
110 people are killed and 63 are missing after a ferry collides with an oil tanker near Dhaka, Bangladesh
19 people are shot dead in a bus attack in Ethiopia
28 people, including 22 children, are killed in a motorway bus crash near Sierre, Switzerland
Encyclopaedia Britannica announces that it will no longer publish printed versions of its encyclopaedia
10 people are killed by a suicide bombing in Kunduz province, Afghanistan
Aleqa Hammond’s Siumut party wins the Greenland parliamentary elections
An Embraer 821 aeroplane crashes and kills 9 people in Para, Brazil
North Korea shreds the Korean Armistice agreement
The European Parliament rejects a European Union budget for the first time
After protests earlier this month, the Israeli parliament votes 65 to 1 for legislation that ends exemptions from military service for ultra-Orthodox Jewish seminary students
Cyclone Pam causes widespread damage in Vanuatu and the South Pacific, including 15-16 deaths.
Suicide Bombing in Ankara, Turkey kills 37 people
Australian Catholic Cardinal George Pell is sentenced to 6 years in prison for sexual abuse in Australia; conviction overturned in 2020
Australian cricketers beat India by 35 runs in Delhi to win ODI series, 3-2; first Australian team to recover from 0-2 in a 5-match series; Usman Khawaja 100, Adam Zampa 3/46
British MPs vote to reject a no-deal Brexit, defeating Theresa May's government 321 votes to 278
Member of the New York Gambino mob family Frank Cali shot dead outside his home, first killing of a high-ranking mobster since 1985
President Trump’s former campaign chairman Paul Manafort sentenced to a further 43 months in jail, to add to his previous 47 months
Shooting at school in Suzano, near São Paulo, Brazil, kills six including five children, before former student gunmen turn guns on themselves
US grounds all Boeing 737 Max aircraft after bans by other countries following the plane type's second crash in Ethiopia
12 hours after PGA Tour cancelled its flagship event, The Players Championship, Augusta National announces postponement of the Masters Tournament to a date to be fixed because of COVID-19 pandemic
Elite football in Britain, including England's Premier League, EFL, Women's Super League plus in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland, is suspended until at least 3 April because of COVID-19 pandemic
US PresidentDonald Trumpdeclares a national emergency, freeing up $50 billion to fight COVID-19
Vinyl records outsell CDs in the US for the first time since 1987 (41 million vinyl records vs 33 million CDs), according to new report
US House of Representatives votes to force TikTok owner China-based ByteDance to sell the social media platform or face a ban in the US
Donatella Versace steps down after 28 years as Chief Creative Officer of Italian fashion house Versace, founded by her brother, with Dario Vitale taking over her role
Battle of Jarnac: French Catholic forces led by Gaspard de Saulx and the Duke of Anjou defeat the Huguenots led by Louis de Bourbon andGaspard de Coligny
William Herschelsees what he thinks is a "comet" but actually discovers the planet Uranus
Confederate PresidentJefferson Davissigns bill authorizing use of slaves as soldiers during last weeks of the US Civil War
Senate begins US PresidentAndrew Johnson's impeachment trial
Alexander IIof Russia is assassinated by members of far-left terror group 'People's Will' who throw a bomb at him in the city of St. Petersburg
Mata Harifirst performs her dance act at the Guimet Museum, Paris
Leon Trotskygains control of the Red Army
Joseph Goebbelsbecomes Nazi Germany's Minister of Information and Propaganda
Failed assassin attempt onAdolf Hitlerduring Smolensk-Rastenburg flight
Nazis liquidate the Jewish ghetto in Kraków;Oskar Schindlerwith advance information, saves his workers by keeping them in his factory overnight
USSR recognizes Italian government ofPietro Badoglio
Dutch monarchQueen Wilhelminareturns to Netherlands at Eede, after five years in exile in the UK
General Motors Corporationreports record net earnings of $656,434,232
Braves' Bobby Thomson breaks his ankle, he is replaced byHank Aaron
Viet Minh GeneralVõ Nguyên Giápopens the assault on French forces at Dien Bien Phu, northwest Vietnam
American western film "The Searchers" is released directed byJohn Fordand starringJohn WayneandNatalie Wood
Bloody battles in Cuba after the student led "Revolutionary Directorate" attacks the presidential palace in Havana in an unsuccessful attempt to depose dictatorFulgencio Batista
Floyd Pattersonovercomes two 1st round knockdowns to KOIngemar Johanssonin 6 in Miami Beach and retain the world heavyweight boxing crown
JFKsets up the Alliance for Progress
Paul HindemithandThornton Wilder's opera "Long Christmas Dinner" premieres in NYC
British guitaristEric Claptonquits the Yardbirds due to the band moving away from traditional blues;Jeff Beckbecomes his replacement
Dennis Lilleetakes 6-26, England all out 95 in Centenary Test
NHL New York IslandersMike Bossy's 5th career hat trick
AmericanJohn Wayne Gacyreceives the death sentence in Illinois for the murder of 12 people
American speed skaterEric Heidensets world record 1000m (1:13.60)
ABC TV crime drama "T.J. Hooker" premieres, starringWilliam Shatner
Ice Dance Championship at Copenhagen won byJayne TorvillandChristopher Dean(Great Britain)
Men's Figure Skating Champions in Copenhagen won byScott Hamilton(USA)
Funeral services held forKonstantin Chernenkoin Moscow
John Gotti, boss of the Gambino crime family, is acquitted of racketeering
Martina Navratilovaand Judy Nelson settle their galamony suit
Australian Federal elections: Australian Labor Party headed byPaul Keatingre-elected for a fifth term
Evander HolyfieldandLennox Lewisfight each other to a split draw after 12 rounds in one of the most controversial decisions in boxing history
Luciano Pavarottiperforms in his final opera, "Tosca," at New York's Metropolitan Opera
Bob Igeris named CEO of Walt Disney International, succeeding Michael Eisner
21st Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees: Black Sabbath; Blondie;Miles Davis; Lynyrd Skynyrd;Sex Pistols;Herb Alpertand Jerry Moss
Argentine Catholic Cardinal Jorge Mario Bergoglio (76) is elected the new pope, taking the papal namePope Francis
"Captain America: The Winter Soldier", directed by Anthony and Joe Russo, starringChris EvansandScarlett Johansson, premieres in Los Angeles, California
National Geographic magazine admits its past coverage was racist in issue to mark 50 years since death ofMartin Luther King Jr.
US Secretary of StateRex Tillersonis fired via a tweet from PresidentDonald Trump
California GovernorGavin Newsomannounces an indefinite moratorium on the death sentence in the state, saying it discriminates against marginalized communities
Cuban-American singer-songwriterGloria Estefanand songwriter-musician Emilio Estefan receive Library of Congress Gershwin Prize at the DAR Constitution Hall in Washington, D.C.
Home of civil rights activists Medgar and Myrlie Evers established as a national monument in Jackson, Mississippi, by PresidentDonald Trump
African AmericanBreonna Taylorshot and killed by police officers executing a no-knock warrant on her flat with a battering ram in Louisville, Kentucky
Microsoft co-founderBill Gatessteps down from the company's board to focus on philanthropic activities
After a 40-day retirement, record breaking quarterbackTom Bradyannounces he will play at least one more season in the NFL with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers
AmericanChester Greenwoodpatents earmuffs after inventing them at age 15
Louis I, Duke of Orléans, Brother of Charles VI of France, born in Paris (d. 1407)
Sasbout Vosmeer, Dutch Catholic theologist and apostole vicar, born in Delft, Netherlands (d. 1614)
William Louis, Count of Nassau, stadtholder of Friesland, leading military commander for the Dutch Republic against the Spanish, born in Dillenburg, Hesse (d. 1620)
Federico Cesi, Italian naturalist and founder of the first modern scientific society, the Accademia dei Lincei, 1603, born in Rome, Italy (d. 1630)
Johannes Berchmans, Dutch Jesuit and Roman Catholic saint, born in Diest, Seventeen Provinces (d. 1621)
Innocent XII [Antonio Pignatelli], Pope (1691-1700), born in Spinazzola, Italy (d. 1700)
John Theophilus Desaguliers, French-British natural philosopher and freemason, born in La Rochelle, France (d. 1744)
Armand de Vignerot du Plessis, French marshal and statesman, 3rd Duke of Richilieu, born in Paris (d. 1788)
James Kent, English composer, born in Winchester, England (d. 1776)
Michel Blavet, French composer, born in Besançon, France (d. 1768)
Isfrid Kayser, German composer, born in Türkheim an der Wertach, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1771)
John Griffin Whitwell, 4th Baron Howard de Walden, British field marshal, born in Oundle, Northamptonshire (d. 1797)
Charles Bonnet, Swiss naturalist and writer, born in Geneva (d. 1793)
Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor (1764-90), born in Schönbrunn Palace, Vienna, Austria (d. 1790)
David Allan, Scottish painter, born in Alloa, Scotland (d. 1796)
Maurus Haberhauer, Czech organist and composer, born in Svitavy, Margraviate of Moravia, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1799)
Guillaume Brune, French marshal, born in Brive-la-Gaillarde, France (d. 1815)
Heinrich Domnich, German horn player and composer, born in Würzburg, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1844)
Charles Louis Willem Josef van Keverberg, Dutch civil servant, born in Haelen, Netherlands (d. 1841)
Daniel Lambert, English gaol keep and famous for his unusually large size (weighed 739 lbs (334 kg) at death), born in Blue Boar Lane, Leicester (d. 1809)
Oliver Shaw, early American composer, born in Newport, Rhode Island (d. 1848)
Karl Friedrich Schinkel, German architect, city planner and painter (Altes Museum, Neue Wache), born in Neuruppin, Margraviate of Brandenburg (d. 1841)
Abigail Fillmore, American 1st lady (1850-53) as the first wife of President Millard Fillmore, born in Stillwater, New York (d. 1853)
Camille Marie Stamaty, French pianist, composer, and teacher of Louis Moreau Gottschalk and Camille Saint-Saëns, born in Département Rome, French Empire (now Rome, Italy) (d. 1870)
James Curtis Hepburn, American missionary and linguist, born in Milton, Pennsylvania (d. 1911)
Albion Parris Howe, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Standish, Maine (d. 1897)
Louis Hébert, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army) and civil engineer, born in Iberville Parish, Louisiana (d. 1901)
Moritz von Strachwitz, German poet, born in Peterwitz, Silesia (d. 1847)
Alberto Randegger, Italian composer, born in Trieste, Austrian Empire (d. 1911)
Emilio Serrano y Ruiz, Spanish composer, born in Vitoria-Gasteiz, Spain (d. 1939)
Maximilien Luce, French painter, born in Paris (d. 1941)
Ivo Bligh, British noble (8th Earl of Darnley) and cricket batsman (4 Tests; first England captain v Australia 1882-83), born in London, England (d. 1927)
Charles Haskell, American lawyer, politician (1st Governor of Oklahoma, 1907-11), and oilman, born in Leipsic, Ohio (d. 1933)
Hugo Wolf, Austrian composer, born in Slovenj Gradec, Slovenia (d. 1903)
Vasiliy Metallov, Russian composer and musical historian, born in Saratov, Russian Empire (d. 1926)
Alexej von Jawlensky, Russian painter, born in Torzhok, Russia (d. 1941)
Albert Meyer, Swiss politician and member of the Swiss Federal Council (1929-38), born in Fällanden, Switzerland (d. 1953)
Oswald Garrison Villard, American journalist and editor of the New York Evening Post, born in Wiesbaden, Germany (d. 1949)
Barbados Joe Walcott, Barbadian boxer (World Welterweight champion 1901-06; first Black to hold that title), born in Demerara, British Guyana (d. 1935)
George Forrest, Scottish botanist (plant hunter in western China), born in Falkirk, Scotland (d. 1932)
Lizzy Ansingh, Dutch painter (Caught Sultane), born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1959)
Balthazar Huibrecht Verhagen, Dutch dramatist and writer, born in Amsterdam (d. 1950)
Enrico Toselli, Italian pianist and composer, born in Florence, Italy (d. 1926)
Emanuel Stickelberger, Swiss writer (Bluthochzeit), born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1962)
Hugh S. Walpole, British novelist and playwright (Jeremy, Maradick at 40), born in Auckland, New Zealand (d. 1941)
Oskar Loerke, German writer (Longest Day-1926), born in Świecie, Poland (d. 1941)
Henri D Gagnebin, Belgian-Swiss organist and composer, born in Liège, Belgium (d. 1977)
John 'Home Run' Baker, American Baseball HOF third baseman (World Series 1910, 11, 13; AL HR leader 1911–14; AL RBI leader 1912, 13; Philadelphia A's), born in Trappe, Maryland (d. 1963)
Carlos Isamitt, Chilean composer, born in Rengo, Chile (d. 1974)
Fritz Busch, German conductor, born in Siegen, Province of Westphalia (d. 1951)
Michael Taube, Polish composer, born in Lodz, Poland (d. 1972)
Alec Rowley, English composer, born in London, England (d. 1958)
Janet Flanner, American journalist (New Yorker), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1978)
Clara Smith, American blues singer, billed as the "Queen of the Moaners", born in Spartanburg County, South Carolina (d. 1935)[1]
Dorothy Aldis, American children's writer, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1966)
Marcel Thiry, Belgian writer and wallon militant, born in Charleroi, Belgium (d. 1977)
Josie Sedgwick, American actress (The Best Man, Queen of the Round-Up), born in Galveston, Texas (d. 1973)
Jan Lechoń [Leszek Józef Serafinowicz], Polish poet, born in Warsaw, Congress Poland (d. 1956)
John Van Vleck, American physicist, mathematician, and Nobel Laureate who pioneered the modern quantum mechanical theory of magnetism, born in Middletown, Connecticut (d. 1980)
Pancho Vladigerov, Bulgarian pianist, composer (Vardar Rhapsody; Song to Peace), and pedagogue, born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1978)[1]
Paul Fix, American actor (Rifleman), born in Dobbs Ferry, New York (d. 1983)
Henry Iliffe Cozens, English Royal Air Force pilot, born in London (d. 1995)
Frank Teschemacher, American jazz clarinetist and alto-saxophonist, born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1932)
Charles Brink, German-born British classical scholar (Horace), born in Berlin (d. 1994)
Frank Wilcox, American actor (Beverly Hillbillies), born in De Soto, Jefferson County, Missouri (d. 1974)
Jack Holden, English athlete (International Cross Country C'ship gold, individual 1933, 34, 35, 39; Empire Games marathon gold 1950), born in Bilston, England (d. 2004)
Maria Pia de Braganca, Portuguese journalist and writer who claimed to be the bastard daughter of King Carlos I of Portugal, born in Lisbon (d. 1995)
Mircea Eliade, Romanian religious historian (Le Yoga) and author, born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 1986) [O.S. 02-28]
Helen Sinclair Glatz (née Hunter), English composer, born in South Shields, Durham, England (d. 1996)
Paul Stewart, American stage, radio, and screen character actor (Citizen Kane; Kiss Me Deadly), director, and producer (The Cavalcade of America), born in Manhattan, New York City (d. 1986)
Walter Annenberg, American publisher (Triangle-TV Guide) and philanthropist, born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 2002)
Karl Gustav Ahlefeldt, Danish actor (Gertrud, Hooray for the Blue Hussars), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1985)
Sammy Kaye [Zarnocay], American big band orchestra leader (Swing and Sway with Sammy Kaye), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1987)
José Ardévol, Spanish-Cuban composer, born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 1981)
L. Ron Hubbard, American sci-fi writer (Death Quest; Dianetics) and founder of Church of Scientology, born in Tilden, Nebraska (d. 1986)
Ernst Hess, Swiss composer, born in Schaffhausen, Switzerland (d. 1968)
Igor Youskevitch, Russian-Ukrainian ballet dancer, born in Pyriatyn, Poltava Oblast, Ukraine (d. 1994)
James Friell, Scottish political cartoonist (Daily Worker), born in Glasgow (d. 1997)
Lambros Konstantaras, Greek actor (O blofatzis, Anna of Rhodes), born in Athen, Greeces (d. 1985)
Lightnin' Slim [Otis Hicks], American Louisiana blues electric guitarist, singer, and songwriter ("Nothing But The Devil";"Bad Luck And Trouble"), born near St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1974)
Reo Stakis, Anglo-Cypriot hotel magnate (Stakis hotels and casinos), born in Kato Drys, Cyprus, (d. 2001)
Sergey Mikhalkov, Soviet writer and poet, born in Moscow (d. 2009) [NS]
Bob Haggart, American dixieland, swing jazz, and session double bass player, and composer ("What's New?"), born in New York City (d. 1998)
Carl-Olof Anderberg, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1972)
Edward O'Hare, American naval aviator of the U.S. Navy, born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1943)
Tessie O'Shea, Welsh stage and screen entertainer, born in Cardiff (d. 1995)
W.O. Mitchell, Canadian writer (Who Has Seen the Wind?), born in Weyburn, Canada (d. 1998)
Ina Ray Hutton [Odessa Cowan], American singer and big band bandleader (Ina Ray Hutton Show), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1984)
Jacque Fresco, American industrial designer and founder of the Venus Project, born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2017)
Lindy Boggs (née Marie Corinne Morrison Claiborne), American politician (US Representative from Louisiana, 1973-97), and diplomat, born in New Roads, Louisiana (d. 2013)
George McAfee, American College-Pro Football HOF utility (Duke University; NFL C'ship 1940, 41, 46; First-team All-Pro & NFL All-Star 1941; Chicago Bears), born in Corbin, Kentucky (d. 2009)
Faye Glenn Abdellah, American pioneer in nursing research and US government official (health services), born in New York City (d. 2017)
Frans van der Elst, Belgian attorney and MP (Volksunie), born in Neder-Over-Heembeek, Belgium (d. 1997)
Ralph J Roberts, American businessman and founder of Comcast, born in New York City (d. 2015)
Al Jaffee, American comic strip cartoonist and illustrator (MAD Magazine, 1955-2020), born in Savannah, Georgia (d. 2023)[1]
Carlo Prosperi, Italian composer (Concerto d'Infanzia; Noi Soldà), and educator, born in Florence, Italy (d. 1990)
Cyril Poole, English cricket batsman (3 Tests, 2 x 50; Nottinghamshire CCC), born in Mansfield, England (d. 1996)
Brun Smith, New Zealand cricket batsman (4 Tests @ 47.40), born in Rangiora, Canterbury (d. 1997)
William F. Bolger, 65th Postmaster General of the United States, born in Waterbury, Connecticut (d. 1989)
Bertha Tickey, American softball pitcher (Hall of Fame 1973), born in Dinuba, California (d. 2014)
Roy Haynes, American jazz drummer (Charlie Parker; Stan Getz; Thelonious Monk; Eric Dolphy; Chick Corea), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2024)[1]
F. H. McClintock, British criminologist, born in London, England (d. 1994)
Lenny Montana [Leonardo Passafaro], American professional wrestler (NWA Georgia), and actor (The Godfather), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 1992)
Charles Sickman Corsen, Dutch Antillean poet, painter, and photographer, born in Curaçao, Dutch  Colony of Curaçao and Dependencies (d. 1994)
Robert Denning, American interior designer, born in New York City (d. 2005)
Ronnie Hazlehurst, English television composer (Are You Being Served?, The Rise and Fall of Reginald Perrin), born in Dukinfield, England (d. 2007)
Bill Cunningham, American fashion photographer (New York Times), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2016)
Jan Howard [Lula Grace Johnson], American country music singer and songwriter (For Loving You), born in West Plains, Missouri (d. 2020)
Jim Slater, British financier and writer, born in Heswall, Cheshire (d. 2015)
Peter Breck, American actor (Black Saddle; Big Valley; Benji), born in Rochester, New York (d. 2012)
(Richard Allen) "Blue" Mitchell, American jazz trumpeter (Horace Silver Quintet; Riverside; Blue Note), born in Miami, Florida (d. 1979)
Doug Harvey, American Baseball HOF umpire (NL 1962-92; 5 x World Series; 7 x MLB All-Star games; career total 4,673 games), born in South Gate, California (d. 2018)
Günther Uecker, German artist known for his reliefs created using nails (Chair (II)), born in Wendorf, Germany (d. 2025)
Marc Dessauvage, Belgian architect, born in Moorslede, Belgium (d. 1984)
Rosalind Elias, American mezzo-soprano (Wagner's Die Walküre - "Grimgerde"; Barber's Vanessa - "Erika"), and opera director, born in Lowell, Massachusetts (d. 2020)
Wolfgang Kohlhaase, German actor, director and writer (Solo Sunday), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2022)
Donald Henry "Pee Wee" Gaskins, American serial killer (claimed to have murdered over 100 people), born in Florence County, South Carolina (d. 1991)
Frank H. Murkowski, American politician (US Senator from Alaska, 1981-2002; Governor of Alaska, 2002-06), born in Seattle, Washington
Gloria McMillan, American radio and television actress (Our Miss Brooks - "Harriet"), born in Portland, Oregon (d. 2022)
